2: In recent years, the European Commission has been rethinking policies related to the environmental impact of products. In this context, the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP), which induced changes in the milk production system, introduced the concept of eco-conditionality, where the allocation of financial support to the dairy sector depends on the fulfilment of certain environ- mental standards. However, all products and services have an environmental impact, whether during their production, use, or disposal, and the exact nature of this impact is complex and diffi- cult to quantify (CEC, 2003). Besides, the need to complement

product policies by considering their entire life cycle is becoming clear. This should ensure that the environmental impact through- out the life cycle is addressed in an integrated approach and not simply shifted from one part of the life cycle to another. It also means that we seek to address these environmental impacts at the time of the life cycle when there is most likely to be a reduc- tion in the overall environmental impacts, in addition to the cost-effective use of resources (CEC, 2003). Life cycle assessment (LCA) is an ISO-standardized (ISO, 2006a,b) environmental accounting framework used to inventory the material and energy inputs and emissions associated with each stage of a product life cycle, from resource extraction to processing, use and disposal and to express these in terms of their quantitative contributions to a specified suite of environmental impact catego- ries (Guinée et al., 2001). Such analysis facilitates the identification

of life cycle stages that contribute disproportionately to specific areas of environmental concern, as well as the comparison of envi- ronmental performance between competing products or produc- tion technologies.

LCA studies have been done on a number of dairy products and, although the system boundaries differed in those studies, one con- sistent finding was that milk production on a dairy farm was responsible for a large part of the environmental impact caused during the life cycle of these products (Berlin, 2002). A LCA study done by Berlin (2002) to analyse the production of cheese in Sweden to the factory gate shows that dairy farms contribute to more than 80% of the global warming, acidification, eutrophication and photochemical ozone creation potentials. In Norway, a LCA study was carried out by Hogaas (2002) on milk production from the dairy farm to the consumer, as well as the waste management of packaging. Hogaas (2002) found that the dairy farm was the greatest contributor to global warming, acidification and eutrophi- cation potentials; the dairy farm and the dairy factory contributed to most of the ozone depletion potential; the life cycle step 'con- sumer/retailer" was a hotspot for photo-oxidant formation and the waste management of packaging was the greatest contributor to the toxicological effects, excluding pesticides. Hospido et al. (2003) applied LCA to the production of milk in Galicia (Spain) to the factory gate and found that dairy farms contribute more towards global warming, eutrophication and acidification poten- tials, whereas dairy factories contribute more towards strato- spheric ozone depletion, photo-oxidant formation and depletion of abiotic resources. LCA studies have also been performed to com- pare alternative farming practices. For example, Cederberg and Mattson (2000) compared organic and conventional milk produc- tion at the farm level in Sweden, while Haas et al. (2001) evaluated intensive, extensified and organic farming in milk production in Germany.

In Portugal, some characteristics of dairy farms and agriculture sectors differ from those of the countries where most of the LCA studies on dairy products were carried out. For instance, milk pro- duction in the Netherlands is 7630 kg of milk per cow (average of a typical conventional and organic dairy farm) (Thomassen et al., 2008), while the milk production per cow in Portugal is lower, averaging approximately 5950 kg (Leitão et al., 2001).

The objective of this study was to assess the environmental impact of the milk production system in mainland Portugal based on LCA methodology, including all processes to the farm gate, in order to identify hotspots in the milk production chain.

6: The LCA methodology used in the present study is consistent with ISO 14040 and 14044 standards (ISO, 2006a,b). According to these standards, LCA consists of four distinct phases: (1) goal and scope definition, (2) inventory analysis, (3) impact assessment and (4) interpretation. The first stage should include: the goal, the functional unit, system boundaries, allocation procedures, assumptions and limitations, among others. In the inventory anal- ysis, a flow model of the technical system is constructed using data on inputs and outputs of resources, energy and emissions to air and water for all activities within the system boundaries. The inventory analysis is followed by impact assessment, in which the inventory data are processed in terms of their environmental impact. This phase includes the following mandatory elements: selection of impact categories and corresponding category indicators and models, classification (assignment of inventory parameters to the impact categories) and characterisation (the inventory results are multiplied by equivalency factors which are specific to each parameter and impact category; thereafter all parameters included

in each impact category are added and the result of the impact cat- egory is obtained). In addition to these mandatory elements, the impact assessment may include the following optional elements: normalisation (calculation of the magnitude of the characterisation results relative to reference information), grouping (sorting and possibly ranking the impact categories) and weighing (conversion and possibly aggregation of the characterisation or normalisation results across impact categories). Interpretation is the phase of LCA in which the findings from the inventory analysis and the im- pact assessment are combined together in order to reach conclu- sions and make recommendations.

8: The objective of this study was to apply the LCA tool in order to quantify the environmental performance of milk production in mainland Portugal to the farm gate, in order to identify the pro- cesses with the largest environmental impact. The system under study (Fig. 1) includes the production of raw milk on Portuguese dairy farms (dairy farm) and the production of forage crops (maize silage and ryegrass silage). The production of forage crops has a connection to dairy farms as the total manure production on the dairy farm is used for forage production resulting in a lower import of fertiliser. Straw and concentrates are produced outside dairy farms and imported into the system. Diesel and electricity produc- tion are also included. As in previous studies, emissions associated with the production of medicines, pesticides, herbicides, machines, buildings and roads were excluded due to the lack of data (Cederberg and Mattson, 2000). The functional unit (FU), i.e., the reference flow to which all other flows in the LCA are related, was the production of one tonne of raw milk ready to be delivered at the farm gate.

Milk production on a dairy farm is a classic example of a multi- functional process. When analysing multifunctional processes, it is important to determine to what extent the environmental burdens of these processes should be allocated to the product researched (Cederberg and Stadig, 2003). In this study, an economic allocation procedure was applied in order to partition the environmental im- pact of the dairy farm among its co-products, milk and meat. Allo- cation was done based on historical market prices of milk and meat in Portugal from 2002 to 2004, and using an average replacement rate of 20% and average calf production, as presented in Fig. 1. The resulting distribution of the associated economic benefits, and of the consequent environmental impact, was 87% for milk and 13% for meat. In the same way, straw is a by-product of cereal produc- tion and due to the assumed non-economic value for straw, the total environmental impact from growing cereal and straw was allocated to cereal.

10: The parameters used to describe the environmental burdens of each process are divided into inputs and outputs. The inputs include raw materials, ancillary materials, fuels and electricity, while the outputs include materials/products, co-products, emis- sions to air and water, and solid waste. Air emissions considered in this study consist of methane (CH4), carbon dioxide (CO2), nitro- gen oxides (NOx), carbon monoxide (CO), nitrous oxide (N20), ammonia (NH3), sulphur dioxide (SO2) and particulates. Water emissions consist of chemical oxygen demand (COD), ammonium (NH), nitrates (NO3) and phosphate (PO).
11: A typical dairy farm was considered for mainland Portugal based on the characteristics of the dairy farms that produce most of the milk in the country (Table 1). The inventory data of the

processes for dairy farms, maize silage, ryegrass silage and straw are shown in Table 2.

The cow's feed on the typical dairy farm consists of forage (maize and ryegrass silage and straw) and concentrates (Leitão et al., 2001), totalling an average daily consumption of 41 kg of feed as fed (16.2 kg dry matter) per livestock unit.

Diesel is consumed by tractors in food distribution and also in the maintenance of stables. Hourly diesel consumption by tractors was estimated based on the linear correlation given by Freitas (2004), where the hourly fuel consumption (litres per hour) is ob- tained by multiplying the power of the tractor (horsepower) by 0.1. Bicudo and Ribeiro (1996) reported daily consumption between 14 and 22 L of water per cow, for systems without pressure washing, and between 27 and 45 L water per cow, for systems with pressure washing, This study assumes that there is an average daily consumption of 27 L of water per cow.

Two types of solid/liquid waste are produced on a dairy farm: manure (wastewater and excrement) and lixiviates. The quantities of waste produced depend on the type of animal, stables (covered or not), diet and husbandry. On the typical dairy farm considered in this study, manure is stored in cesspits and does not undergo any type of treatment, resulting in a total amount of manure stored yearly of approximately 25 m³ per cow. After temporary storage, all the manure was considered to be spread on agricultural land. The production of lixiviates resulting from the fermentation pro- cesses that occur during the ensiling of forage was assumed to be insignificant due to the high level of dry matter of maize and ryegrass.

Total air emissions are the sum of the emissions generated by the tractors with diesel engines, the emissions from enteric fer- mentation of the cows and the emissions from manure manage- ment (manure handling and storage). Air emissions generated by the use of tractors with diesel engines were estimated based on emission factors provided by Borken et al. (1999). The annual emis- sion of CH4 was considered to be 109 kg CH4 per dairy cow and 57 kg CH4 per calf from enteric fermentation and 37 kg CH4 per dairy cow and 11 kg CH4 per calf from manure management (IPCC, 2006). Emissions of N20 may occur directly and indirectly during manure management (IPCC, 2006). Direct emissions of N20 from manure occur when the nitrogen contained in manure is nitrified or denitrified and these were determined by multiplying the total amount of nitrogen excreted (0.48 kg N per 1000 kg from dairy cows and 0.33 kg N per 1000 kg from calves) by the emission factor specific for a management system where manure is stored as a liquid in a tank (0.005 kg N20-N per kg N) and by the typical weight of dairy cows and calves (IPCC, 2006). Indirect emissions of N20 result from volatile nitrogen losses that occur primarily in the forms of NH3 and NOx and these were considered to be insignif- icant. Nitrogen volatilisation was considered to occur in the form of NH3. In this case, NH3 emissions were obtained by multiplying the amount of nitrogen excreted by the fraction of volatile nitrogen (40%) (IPCC, 2006). Since the manure is spread onto the soil where the forage crops are produced, direct N20 emissions and NH3 emis- sions from managed soils are analysed in the forage production inventory.

22: Forage production consists of the cultivation of maize and rye- grass and their preservation in the form of silage (Leitão et al., 2001). It is normal practice in Portugal to cultivate these crops alternately in the same soil. Production of maize silage is com- prised of the following steps: soil preparation, fertilisation, pesti- cide application, irrigation, harvesting and ensiling. Production of ryegrass includes the stages of soil preparation, sowing, harvesting and ensiling. The inventory data for these processes are presented in the Table 2.

The consumption of diesel associated with all stages of maize and ryegrass silage production was obtained using the same proce- dure as that applied to estimate diesel consumption by tractors on the dairy farm. Table 3 presents the type of operations and equip- ment used, the respective hourly diesel consumption rates, and the actual work time of the equipment. The emission factors associated with diesel combustion in these operations were taken from Borken et al. (1999).

All manure produced on the dairy farm is applied to the soil during soil preparation for maize and ryegrass production. This is a result of both a small storage capacity for manure on farms and the lack of integrated treatment systems for manure. Therefore, of the total amount of manure produced on a typical Portuguese dairy farm (105.71 m³ per ha and per year), half was assumed to be applied to ryegrass production in September/October and the other half to maize production in April/May. For the ryegrass silage no artificial fertilisers are required because nutrient needs are fully met through manure application. In maize production, artificial fertilisers are needed in addition to manure application. The pro- duction characteristics for maize and ryegrass silage considered in this study were established based on Dias et al. (2003) and Lopes

et al. (2006), respectively, and are shown in Table 4. The inventory data for production of fertilisers used in the production of maize refer to average values of fertiliser production in eight Dutch industries in 1993 (IDEMAT, 2001). However, the original data regarding electricity consumption were adjusted by using the Por- tuguese electricity mix instead of the Dutch electricity mix.

No water consumption was considered for the ryegrass planta- tion because ryegrass is an autumn-winter crop and it is assumed that the precipitation during this period is enough to guarantee its water requirements. An average water consumption of 5580 m³ per ha was assumed for maize irrigation (DGADR, 2007). Electricity

Direct emissions to the atmosphere resulting from the applica- tion of manure and artificial fertilisers to soil were calculated con- sidering that 1% of the total nitrogen applied to soil is released as N20 (IPCC, 2006). NH3 emissions to air resulting from nitrogen vol- atilisation were obtained by multiplying the amount of nitrogen applied to the soil (in artificial fertilisers and manure) by the cor- responding rates of volatilisation, 0.1 kg NH3-N per kg of N con- tained in the fertiliser applied and 0.2 kg NH3-N per kg of N contained in manure applied or deposited (IPCC, 2006).

Water emissions consist of NO3 and PO NO3 emissions were quantified by assuming that 0.3 kg of nitrogen (as NO3) is lost through leaching/runoff per kg of N contained in the fertiliser (both artificial and manure) applied to soil (IPCC, 2006). To estimate PO emissions, it was considered that 2.2% (Thyo and Wenzel, 2007) and 5.35% (Dalgaard and Halberg, 2002) of the phosphorus added to soil in the production of maize and ryegrass, respectively, are discharged into the water as PO
25: Forage production consists of the cultivation of maize and rye- grass and their preservation in the form of silage (Leitão et al., 2001). It is normal practice in Portugal to cultivate these crops alternately in the same soil. Production of maize silage is com- prised of the following steps: soil preparation, fertilisation, pesti- cide application, irrigation, harvesting and ensiling. Production of ryegrass includes the stages of soil preparation, sowing, harvesting and ensiling. The inventory data for these processes are presented in the Table 2.

The consumption of diesel associated with all stages of maize and ryegrass silage production was obtained using the same proce- dure as that applied to estimate diesel consumption by tractors on the dairy farm. Table 3 presents the type of operations and equip- ment used, the respective hourly diesel consumption rates, and the actual work time of the equipment. The emission factors associated with diesel combustion in these operations were taken from Borken et al. (1999).

All manure produced on the dairy farm is applied to the soil during soil preparation for maize and ryegrass production. This is a result of both a small storage capacity for manure on farms and the lack of integrated treatment systems for manure. Therefore, of the total amount of manure produced on a typical Portuguese dairy farm (105.71 m³ per ha and per year), half was assumed to be applied to ryegrass production in September/October and the other half to maize production in April/May. For the ryegrass silage no artificial fertilisers are required because nutrient needs are fully met through manure application. In maize production, artificial fertilisers are needed in addition to manure application. The pro- duction characteristics for maize and ryegrass silage considered in this study were established based on Dias et al. (2003) and Lopes

No water consumption was considered for the ryegrass planta- tion because ryegrass is an autumn-winter crop and it is assumed that the precipitation during this period is enough to guarantee its water requirements. Direct emissions to the atmosphere resulting from the applica- tion of manure and artificial fertilisers to soil were calculated con- sidering that 1% of the total nitrogen applied to soil is released as N20 (IPCC, 2006). NH3 emissions to air resulting from nitrogen vol- atilisation were obtained by multiplying the amount of nitrogen applied to the soil (in artificial fertilisers and manure) by the cor- responding rates of volatilisation, 0.1 kg NH3-N per kg of N con- tained in the fertiliser applied and 0.2 kg NH3-N per kg of N contained in manure applied or deposited (IPCC, 2006).

Water emissions consist of NO3 and PO NO3 emissions were quantified by assuming that 0.3 kg of nitrogen (as NO3) is lost through leaching/runoff per kg of N contained in the fertiliser (both artificial and manure) applied to soil (IPCC, 2006). 31: The animal feed in the form of concentrate mixtures is pro- duced industrially by mixing a variety of ingredients. The compo- sition of the concentrates was defined based on the knowledge of experts in animal nutrition (Table 5). The concentrate production data were taken from Nielsen et al. (2003). These data refer to 2001 and the main production processes are: receiving and storing ingredients, mixing and grinding the ingredients, kneading the mixture in pellets, drying, cooling and storing pellets for distribu- tion. No wastewater is generated during production. The environ- mental burdens associated with the production of concentrate ingredients were also included in this process. Inventory data for molasses (a by-product of sugar production) was obtained in the LCA Food database (Nielsen et al., 2003). Inventory data used for maize grain production were the same as those used for maize si- lage production, with some adjustments in relation to productivity and harvest processes. For the production of barley, wheat, rye and soybeans, the inventory data adopted were from Dalgaard and Hal- berg (2002). These data are based on Danish production conditions which could differ from current Portuguese production conditions (for example, the amount of fertiliser/manure used depends on na- tional regulation). Inventory data for corn gluten meal were de- rived from BUWAL (1996) considering that corn gluten meal is a coproduct of corn starch production.

2.2.5. Fuels end electricity The fuels used in the various processes for traction and heating are diesel (dairy farm, maize and ryegrass silage production, straw production, and concentrate ingredient production), naphtha (con- centrate production), coal and natural gas (fertiliser production).

36: In this study, the impact assessment was carried out up to the characterisation phase, as the optional phases were not necessary to identify the hotspots of the system. The impact categories se- lected for this study were: abiotic depletion, global warming, pho- tochemical oxidation, acidification and eutrophication. Other impact categories usually considered in LCA studies (e.g., ozone layer depletion, human toxicity) were not addressed due to the lack of inventory data. The inventory parameters assigned to each impact category as well as the characterisation factors used in this study are all presented in Table 6.
37: Fig. 2 shows the relative and absolute contribution of each pro- cess to air and water emissions resulting from the production of one tonne of raw milk on mainland Portugal's dairy farms.

The activities on the dairy farm contribute significantly to the total emissions of NH3 (74%) and CH4 (99%), mainly due to enteric fermentation and manure production and management. The dairy enterprise also has an important role in NO emissions, being responsible for about 26% of the total NO emissions, mainly due to the burning of diesel in the mechanical equipment used for food distribution and stable maintenance. Maize silage and concentrate production also have relevant contributions to NOx emissions (25% each one) resulting from fuel burning.
Concentrate production is the main source of CO2, CO, N20, particulate, SO2 and PO emissions, with contributions ranging from 34% to 79% of the total emissions of these substances. Most

of the CO2 and CO emissions are caused by gluten production, whereas N20 emissions are mainly due to the processing of soy- beans to produce soy meal. The major part of particulate and SO2 emissions are related to the production of gluten and soy meal (de- rived from the consumption of electricity and fuel in boilers). The majority of PO emissions into water result from concentrate pro- duction and are related to the application of fertilisers and manure to the soil during cultivation of concentrate ingredients. Phospho- rous contained in fertilisers and manure added to the soil is lost in the form of PO by leaching. It is important to note that PO emissions are almost exclusively released during cattle feed pro- duction, which besides concentrate production also includes maize and ryegrass production. The same is true for NO3 emissions to water, although in this case the production of maize silage is the largest contributor to total emissions (43%). The application of fer- tilisers and manure onto the soil is the main reason for this contri- bution, followed by the emissions of wastewater from the production of artificial fertilisers.
The production of diesel consumed on the dairy farm is the main emission source of COD and NH4 to bodies of water, account- ing for 31% and 32% of the total emissions of COD and NH, respectively.

The abiotic depletion potential from the production of one tonne of raw milk in Portugal is approximately 1.4 kg Sb eq. Concentrate production is responsible for 0.48 kg Sb eq. due to the consumption of electricity and fossil fuels, both from the indus- trial boilers and the agricultural equipment used in the cultivation of the concentrate ingredients. Similarly, Hospido et al. (2003) identified that concentrate production is the major contributor to the abiotic depletion potential of Galician milk production at the farm level. Maize silage production also has an important contribu- tion to abiotic depletion (28%) that results from the use of fuel in mechanized equipment. About 60% of the total abiotic depletion potential is associated with the depletion of oil, whereas about 22% and 18% of this potential is associated with the depletion of coal and natural gas, respectively.

The global warming potential resulting from the production of one tonne of milk is approximately 1021 kg CO2 eq. Around 718 kg CO2 eq. originate from dairy farms, mainly due to CH4 emit- ted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The pro- duction of concentrates, maize and ryegrass silage represent, globally, around 27% of the total global warming potential caused essentially by CO2 and N20 emissions. Casey and Holden (2005) also concluded that enteric fermentation and manure management were responsible for a large part (60%) of the global warming potential obtained in the average Irish milk production system. The emissions of CH4, CO2 and N20 contribute to 64%, 19% and 17%, respectively, of the total global warming potential. Cederberg and Mattson (2000) and Hogaas (2002) also show that CH4, which is emitted mostly from the cattle, is the major contributor to total global warming on dairy farms, about 50-65%. De Boer (2003) also reported a contribution of 48-65% for CH4 from a number of stud- ies on organic and conventional milk production systems in Germany, Sweden and the Netherlands.

The photochemical oxidation potential from the production of one tonne of milk amounted to 0.19 kg C2H4 eq. The production of raw milk on dairy farms is responsible for 0.16 kg C2H4 eq., mainly due to CH4 emissions from manure management and enteric fermentation. Hospido et al. (2003) also found that the activities on dairy farms make the most contribution to the photo- chemical oxidation potential in Galician milk production (68%).

Concentrate and maize silage production represent 8% and 5%, respectively, of the total photochemical oxidation potential. These contributions are derived essentially from SO2 emitted from the burning of fossil fuels for processing this feed. Of the total photo- chemical oxidation potential, 82% is due to CH4 emissions, 12% is due to SO2 emissions and 6% is due to CO emissions.

The acidification potential is approximately 20 kg SO2 eq. per tonne of milk produced. About 13.9 kg SO2 eq. are attributed to dairy farm activities, mainly as a result of NH3 emissions from vol- atilization of the nitrogen contained in manure. Haas et al. (2001) also shows that the acidification potential is almost exclusively caused by NH3 emissions from cattle keeping. Maize and ryegrass silage production contribute to 14% and 10% of the global acidifi- cation potential, also mainly due to the NH3 emissions resulting from the volatilisation of the nitrogen contained in manure and fertilisers applied to the soils. Globally, NH3 emissions are respon- sible for 87% of the total acidification potential, whereas SO2 and NOX have minor contributions of 9% and 4%, respectively. NH3 was also the emission that accounted for most of the total acidifi- cation potential of milk production at farm level in the Nether- lands, with a contribution of 74% and 81% in conventional and organic systems, respectively (Thomassen et al., 2008). Concentrate production is responsible for 0.48 kg Sb eq. due to the consumption of electricity and fossil fuels, both from the indus- trial boilers and the agricultural equipment used in the cultivation of the concentrate ingredients. Similarly, Hospido et al. (2003) identified that concentrate production is the major contributor to the abiotic depletion potential of Galician milk production at the farm level. Maize silage production also has an important contribu- tion to abiotic depletion (28%) that results from the use of fuel in mechanized equipment. About 60% of the total abiotic depletion potential is associated with the depletion of oil, whereas about 22% and 18% of this potential is associated with the depletion of coal and natural gas, respectively.

The global warming potential resulting from the production of one tonne of milk is approximately 1021 kg CO2 eq. Around 718 kg CO2 eq. originate from dairy farms, mainly due to CH4 emit- ted from enteric fermentation and manure management. The pro- duction of concentrates, maize and ryegrass silage represent, globally, around 27% of the total global warming potential caused essentially by CO2 and N20 emissions. Casey and Holden (2005) also concluded that enteric fermentation and manure management were responsible for a large part (60%) of the global warming potential obtained in the average Irish milk production system. The emissions of CH4, CO2 and N20 contribute to 64%, 19% and 17%, respectively, of the total global warming potential. Cederberg and Mattson (2000) and Hogaas (2002) also show that CH4, which is emitted mostly from the cattle, is the major contributor to total global warming on dairy farms, about 50-65%. De Boer (2003) also reported a contribution of 48-65% for CH4 from a number of stud- ies on organic and conventional milk production systems in Germany, Sweden and the Netherlands.

The photochemical oxidation potential from the production of one tonne of milk amounted to 0.19 kg C2H4 eq. The production of raw milk on dairy farms is responsible for 0.16 kg C2H4 eq., mainly due to CH4 emissions from manure management and enteric fermentation. Hospido et al. (2003) also found that the activities on dairy farms make the most contribution to the photo- chemical oxidation potential in Galician milk production (68%).

Concentrate and maize silage production represent 8% and 5%, respectively, of the total photochemical oxidation potential. These contributions are derived essentially from SO2 emitted from the burning of fossil fuels for processing this feed. Of the total photo- chemical oxidation potential, 82% is due to CH4 emissions, 12% is due to SO2 emissions and 6% is due to CO emissions.

About 13.9 kg SO2 eq. are attributed to dairy farm activities, mainly as a result of NH3 emissions from vol- atilization of the nitrogen contained in manure. Haas et al. The processes related to the production of cattle feed (maize and ryegrass silage and concentrates) also have important contribu- tions to this impact category, mainly due to emissions of NO3 resulting from the processing and application of fertilisers and manure to soil. It should be noted that cattle feed production as a whole releases 4.1 kg PO eq. per tonne of milk produced (58% of the total eutrophication potential). In the case of Galician milk production, concentrate production contributed the most to the eutrophication potential (Hospido et al., 2003). Almost 58% of the total eutrophication potential is associated with NH3 emissions,
followed by the emission of NO3 (35%). The remaining parameters make minor contributions to this impact category.
50: In this study, the processes associated with the production of milk up to the farm gate in mainland Portugal were identified and characterised, and their resource consumption and emission of pollutants and related environmental impacts were quantified.

The inventory results suggest that most of the emissions to air and water bodies were fundamentally caused by cattle feed pro- duction, in particular concentrates and maize silage. On the other hand, the activities of the dairy enterprise were the major source of NOX (from fuel combustion), NH3 and CH4 (from manure man- agement and enteric fermentation). Diesel production played a major role in NH + and COD emissions. The results from the impact assessment indicate that the activities carried out on dairy farms (manure management, enteric fermentation and diesel consump- tion) played a major role in the selected impact categories, except in abiotic depletion. Thus, while cattle feed production is responsi- ble for more emissions to air and water; the activities carried out on dairy farms generate a larger potential environmental impact, primarily due to manure management.

In this context, it is important to make technological and struc- tural changes to Portuguese dairy farms in order to lessen their environmental impact. Possible improvement opportunities that should be further researched require the implementation of inte- grated solutions for manure recovery/treatment (e.g. anaerobic digestion with biogas recovery for energy production and digestate valorisation as fertiliser) that could potentially contribute to a de- crease of CH4, N20 and NH3 emissions and, consequently, to creat- ing less impact with regards to global warming, eutrophication, acidification and photochemical oxidation.

Cattle feed production as a whole (maize and ryegrass silage, straw and concentrates) was found to be a hotspot for the abiotic depletion and eutrophication impact categories, representing more than 50% of the total impact of these categories. Further work is needed in order to analyse possible opportunities for decreasing the environmental impact of maize and ryegrass, namely: (i) sub- mitting manure to treatment (e.g. anaerobic digestion) before its application on soil as organic fertiliser; (ii) improving manure nutrient use efficiencies in order to decrease the import of nutri- ents; and (iii) diversifying feeding crops, as the dependence on two annual forages crops, produced in the same area (maize and ryegrass) is expected to lead to excessive soil mobilization (and re- lated impacts) and to insignificant CO2 sequestration from the atmosphere. In contrast, in a system where several crops are culti- vated, including perennial crops for grazing, soil mobilisation is ex- pected to be less intensive and CO2 sequestration is expected to be higher. Another important issue that needs to be considered in fur- ther work is the effect of changing the concentrate mixture in the environmental performance of milk production.

The improvement of the environmental performance of this sector can contribute to consolidation in the national market and to expansion into new markets. Thus, tools such as life cycle assessment should be of widespread use in the identification and implementation of development strategies and marketing.
